did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models (CNN with ResNet18 backbone, DenseNet201, and UNet) combined with Explainable AI (XAI) techniques like Grad-CAM and attention modules (SE, CBAM) to classify skin lesions (melanoma, atypical nevus, common nevus) from dermoscopic images using the PH2 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing and evaluating three deep learning models (CNN, DenseNet201, UNet) for skin lesion classification using the PH2 dataset. It includes specific sections on Methods and Materials, Results with quantitative performance metrics (accuracy, F1-score, AUC), and a comparative analysis, indicating it is an original technical study rather than a review.

title: Skin Lesion Classification with Explainable AI to Enhancing Dermatological Diagnosis
brief_summary: This study developed three deep learning models—CNN (ResNet18), DenseNet201, and UNet—to classify skin lesions (melanoma, atypical nevus, and common nevus) from dermoscopic images using the PH2 dataset. To address interpretability and performance challenges, the models were enhanced with attention modules (SE, CBAM), data augmentation (Mixup), and Explainable AI techniques like Grad-CAM. The UNet model achieved the highest accuracy of 95%, outperforming DenseNet201 (93%) and CNN (90%). Grad-CAM visualizations confirmed that the models focused on relevant lesion features, thereby enhancing clinical trust and transparency. The results demonstrate that combining deep learning with XAI provides a reliable and accurate tool for automated melanoma diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three classes in the PH2 dataset used for classification. The study highlights its importance as a life-threatening skin cancer and notes high AUC (1.00) and recall (0.99) for this class in the UNet model.
One of the three classes in the PH2 dataset. The paper notes confusion between atypical nevus and melanoma due to visual similarity, and mentions specific AUC (0.99) for this class in the UNet model.

One of the three classes in the PH2 dataset. The paper notes that some uncommon nevi were misclassified into common nevi by DenseNet201, and mentions specific AUC (0.99) for this class in the UNet model.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion classes (melanoma, atypical nevus, common nevus)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the objective is 'skin lesion classification' and 'melanoma prediction' using discrete diagnostic categories. The models (CNN, DenseNet201, UNet) output class probabilities for three distinct classes from the PH2 dataset. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC-ROC for class discrimination.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image dataset collected from Kaggle, containing images of skin lesions. The study used this dataset for training and testing deep learning models for skin lesion classification.

use.training: Used for training the CNN, DenseNet201, and UNet models after data augmentation (generating 10 augmented copies per image via flipping, rotation, and color jittering) and resizing to 224x224.
use.testing: Used for testing model performance with an 80/20 train-test split. The testing set consisted of 88 images total (16 per class).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To increase reliability by checking the model on multiple segments of data and checking the efficacy and generalisation ability of the model for clinical use.
To evaluate model performance, specifically to identify misclassifications (e.g., uncommon nevi misclassified into common nevi) and assess class-wise performance.
To measure the model’s ability to discriminate between high-risk and low-risk lesions and to report Area Under the Curve (AUC) scores.
To evaluate model performance using metrics such as precision, recall, and F1-score.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC-ROC, along with confusion matrices and ROC curves, without statistical significance testing between models.

PH2 Dataset.notes: The UNet model achieved the highest accuracy (95%) and F1-scores across all classes. DenseNet201 showed well-balanced performance due to Mixup and Focal Loss. CNN had more confusion between Class 0 (atypical nevus) and Class 1 (common nevus). Most errors occurred between atypical nevus and melanoma due to visual similarity. Some uncommon nevi were misclassified into common nevi by DenseNet201. The study used an 80/20 train-test split on the PH2 dataset.

architecture_used.main_model: Three models: CNN (ResNet18 backbone), DenseNet201, and UNet
architecture_used.pretraining: DenseNet201 employed a pre-trained TensorFlow model with custom top layers imported from Keras. UNet used a DenseNet-style encoder. CNN used a ResNet18 backbone.

enhancements: Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) blocks for channel-wise feature refinement
Multi-Scale Feature Fusion (MSFF) mechanism combining high- and mid-level features via 1x1 convolutions, bilinear upsampling, and channel-wise concatenation
CNN.classification_head: Redesigned with a dropout layer (probability 0.7), a fully connected layer of 512 units with ReLU activation and batch normalization, and a final layer outputting three logits.

enhancements: Focal Loss for handling class imbalance
DenseNet201.loss_function: Focal Loss: FL(pt) = -αt(1 - pt)^γ log(pt), with γ set to 2.

UNet.decoder: Decoder with UpConv layers and skip connections
enhancements: Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) applied after the encoder for channel and spatial attention

training_details.learning_rate_schedule: Adaptive OneCycleLR scheduling with cosine annealing
training_details.epochs: 50 epochs for general description; UNet specifically trained for 40 epochs with early stopping
training_details.data_augmentation: Mixup (generating synthetic samples by combining training pairs), flipping, rotation, and color jittering (10 augmented copies per image)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: CNN (ResNet18 backbone with Squeeze-and-Excitation blocks and Multi-Scale Feature Fusion)
DenseNet201 (with Squeeze-and-Excitation blocks and Focal Loss)
UNet (with DenseNet-style encoder, Convolutional Block Attention Module, and Grad-CAM)
preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation: Generating 10 augmented copies per image via flipping, rotation, and color jittering
Train-test split: 80/20 split applied to the dataset

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Flipping, rotation, and color jittering were applied to generate 10 augmented copies of every image in the PH2 dataset.
Used as a data enhancement technique to generate synthetic samples by combining training pairs, specifically employed in the DenseNet201 model to handle class imbalance and improve generalization.

conclusions: The UNet model with CBAM and Grad-CAM achieved the highest accuracy (95%) and F1-scores across all lesion classes, outperforming DenseNet201 (93%) and CNN (90%).

Combining deep learning models with Explainable AI (XAI) techniques like Grad-CAM and attention modules (SE, CBAM) enhances both classification accuracy and interpretability, thereby increasing clinical trust and transparency.

Grad-CAM visualizations confirmed that the models focused on relevant lesion features, with the UNet model producing attention maps along lesion boundaries that resembled dermatologist-like attention.

Most misclassifications occurred between atypical nevi and melanoma due to their visual similarity, although the UNet model demonstrated strong performance in detecting high-risk melanoma cases (AUC 1.00).
Techniques such as Mixup data augmentation and Focal Loss helped DenseNet201 achieve well-balanced performance and handle class imbalance effectively.

The study demonstrates that integrating attention mechanisms, advanced augmentation, and XAI provides a reliable, accurate, and transparent tool for automated melanoma diagnosis, potentially enhancing clinical decision-making.
The UNet model showed good generalization with low test loss variability and no significant overfitting, while the CNN model exhibited a larger difference between training and testing accuracy.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the small PH2 dataset (440 images total), which limits the generalizability of the results to broader, more diverse clinical populations.

There is a discrepancy in the reported dataset distribution: the text states 80/20 train-test splits (352/88 images), but Table I lists different counts for augmented and testing sets (e.g., 181 testing for atypical nevus), creating ambiguity about the exact test set size and composition.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (p-values) to compare the performance differences between the models, relying instead on descriptive metrics.
The UNet model was trained for only 40 epochs with early stopping, while other models were described with 50 epochs, potentially introducing a training duration bias in the comparison.

The PH2 dataset is known to have class imbalance (e.g., only 40 melanoma cases), which the authors acknowledge but mitigate only through augmentation and loss functions rather than external validation.
The study lacks external validation on independent datasets (e.g., HAM10000 or ISIC), limiting the evidence for real-world clinical deployment robustness.
Some errors in the CNN model involved focusing on irrelevant background areas rather than the lesion, indicating potential issues with attention mechanism efficacy in that specific architecture.
The paper is a conference publication (MECON 2025) with no funding support, which may imply limited resources for extensive validation or ablation studies.
